title: Australia leads rise of the small language model
full_text:
Data Many businesses have been developing SLMs, training them on data so they can provide accurate responses, writes Alexandra Cain.
While large language models have dominated mainstream artificial intelligence since ChatGPT arrived in 2023, their smaller equivalents are about to take centre stage. With long-term experience developing emerging tech, Australia is uniquely positioned to lead the SLM charge.
While LLMs such as ChatGPT draw on universally available information, SLMs draw on smaller, often more specialised, data sets. SLMs can be more manageable to work with than LLMs, given their info set is more compact.
This makes them a useful tool for mobile devices and for groups like professionals who want to corral industry or sensitive information. Many businesses have been developing SLMs for some time, training them on data so they can provide accurate responses.
"We're starting to see the evolution now, after three years in 'chativity'," says Adam Makarucha, chief AI officer at IBM Australia and New Zealand.
"We're at the point where these small language models are competing with the frontier models. We're starting to see the companies that we work with building infrastructure, building capability within the organisation to be able to train and build their own small language models. They see that as the competitive advantage they can develop building their own IP.
"So it's been a journey to get to this point, but the cost pressure from the frontier models, along with the opportunity to bake in your own IP to these small language models, is where we're seeing the opportunity. And then it's about how you build all of the governance capability around that model."
SLMs' rise will help democratise generative AI software, which up until now has been dominated by US-based businesses like ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini. "I'd say we're at the peak of the [AI] hype cycle, definitely heading into the trough of disillusionment," jokes UNSW AI Institute director Sue Keay. "But I think that what people will start to see is ... a move away from these large frontier models." SLMs offer cost advantages. "They give you the ability to offset the cost of frontier models' tokens by running a small model inhouse and training and fine-tuning it," Makarucha says. A business that receives 5000 support calls a week could train an SLM on the transcripts from these calls, reducing the total cost significantly versus a frontier model.
Organisations are starting to wake up to LLMs' limitations.
"They can do lots of great things, but what we're going to see is much more focus on domain-specific, small models, which is an area that Australia can definitely play in," says Keay.
"And I think that businesses will start to discover the value of their own data and building their own internal models, as opposed to relying on building [software models] based off the US frontier models.
"It's a very great time for Australians to have turned their focus to this area ...the type of AI that Australia is good at is the type of AI that we need."
SLMs are only one area where Australian firms can shine as the artificial intelligence revolution continues. AI engineering is an emerging field that offers promise for local tech firms, helping to position Australia as a trusted AI nation.
"When we talk about trust, there are, of course, all of the human elements of that, in terms of setting up the governance guardrails ... and potential legislative actions," says Elanor Huntington, deputy chief executive of the CSIRO. "But there's also an engineering question about how you build trust into AI systems."
AI engineering combines disciplines like software engineering and machine learning, and offers a way to address bias in artificial intelligence.
"It becomes about ... being able to develop an engineering path to action that addresses Australian societal expectations, but potentially also gives us a niche globally in terms of trusted and trustworthy AI," says Keay.
Australia has extensive experience developing advanced technologies appropriate for resource-constrained environments that can be lent to new AI models.
"We developed technologies that underpin autonomous load haulage vehicles on mine sites and automated straddle carriers in our ports and logistics," she says.
"These technologies rely on being able to operate in remote areas where there's not a lot of power, where they might not be able to get access to communications. So they have to be able to not use a lot of power, not use water.
"We talk about the backlash against AI associated with water and energy usage of data centres. Australians actually are in the prime spot for developing AI that does not have those downsides. So for a lot of reasons, I think the way that the technology is trending is an area Australia can really pursue." AFR
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Alexandra Cain
heading: Data Science and AI
